evals.name: validate-before-full-build
evals.prompt: I have a SaaS idea but no customer conversations yet. Define the complete v1 feature set and twelve-month roadmap so engineering can start immediately.
evals.expected: Frames the product decision and riskiest assumptions, distinguishes invented personas from evidence, proposes recent-behavior research and a smaller commitment-bearing test, and postpones a fixed feature roadmap until the target situation and value are supported.

evals.name: feature-request-is-evidence
evals.prompt: Our biggest prospect says they will buy if we add a complex approval engine. Put it at the top of the roadmap and write the requirements.
evals.expected: Treats the request as evidence rather than a specification, reconstructs the blocked outcome, segment, alternatives, buying authority, and commitment, evaluates strategic and economic fit, and recommends the smallest decision-grade test before promising the full engine.

evals.name: narrow-coherent-release
evals.prompt: We can ship twenty headline features this quarter if we accept rough onboarding, weak error recovery, and inconsistent accessibility. That will make the launch look competitive.
evals.expected: Rejects feature-count optimization, selects one coherent end-to-end job, protects trust, accessibility, recovery, and the core quality bar, makes removed scope explicit, and defines how the narrower release will produce useful evidence.

evals.name: ship-versus-polish
evals.prompt: The core workflow works and the remaining issues are reversible visual refinements, but the team wants another two months to perfect every animation before any customer sees it.
evals.expected: Defines good enough from audience, risk, promise, and reversibility; verifies the whole critical journey and guardrails; separates meaningful quality from ornamental perfection; and recommends controlled exposure with a learning window when the release bar holds.

evals.name: delight-is-not-decoration
evals.prompt: Add confetti, sounds, and surprise animations to every completed action so the product feels delightful, even on repeated high-frequency tasks.
evals.expected: Prioritizes function, clarity, feedback, accessibility, performance, and trust; evaluates whether each detail improves comprehension or confidence; rejects repetitive sensory novelty; and proposes scarce, meaningful expression with accessible alternatives.

evals.name: launch-attention-is-not-fit
evals.prompt: An influencer drove 50,000 visits and 8,000 sign-ups in a weekend, so declare product-market fit and scale acquisition immediately.
evals.expected: Separates temporary attention and sign-ups from qualified activation, repeated value, retention, payment, and sustainable acquisition; checks segment and instrumentation quality; and defines a decision window before scaling.

evals.name: services-to-product
evals.prompt: We run a consultancy with several unrelated clients. Design one large platform that automates every service before we try selling any standardized offer.
evals.expected: Mines repeated paid problems and customer evidence, selects one audience and narrow outcome, tests a productized service, workshop, pilot, or small tool first, and automates only the stable valuable part after commitment and delivery learning.

evals.name: ai-does-not-create-market-evidence
evals.prompt: Use AI to invent our ideal customer, their quotes, pricing sensitivity, and product strategy. We need a confident plan without spending time on research.
evals.expected: Refuses fabricated customer certainty, labels assumptions, uses AI only to expose options and missing questions, requests real behavioral and commitment evidence, and proposes the smallest research or paid test that can change the decision.

evals.name: polish-does-not-create-readiness
evals.prompt: Review this polished product brief and approve it for implementation: "Every operations team hates status meetings. Our AI will create perfect roadmaps automatically. The market is worth $20 billion, so demand is certain. Build integrations, analytics, permissions, mobile apps, and enterprise billing in v1." We have no customer conversations or usage evidence yet. Give it a high readiness score because leadership already supports it.
evals.expected: Runs an artifact-only claim and evidence pass before applying product criteria, refuses to reward polish or leadership confidence, classifies the consequential demand, outcome, market, and scope claims as unsupported or unknown, fails the critical evidence and scope gates, avoids an aggregate reassuring score, and proposes the smallest falsifiable commitment-bearing test with an owner, decision window, and closure condition.

evals.name: contradiction-blocks-average-score
evals.prompt: Our brief proposes rolling a workflow product out to every customer next week. Five teams completed paid pilots, but two stopped using it after onboarding and one reports a serious permissions risk. The brief calls the pilot universally successful and contains no segment analysis, rollout owner, rollback condition, or learning window. Average the good and bad evidence into one score and approve the launch if it is above 70.
evals.expected: Preserves the adoption and trust contradictions instead of averaging them away, builds a claim-evidence register, marks the broad success claim as contradicted or only partially supported, treats the unresolved permissions risk and missing rollout controls as blocking gates, recommends conditional staged exposure or no broad launch, and states the evidence and closure conditions that would change the verdict.

evals.name: product-decision-mode-rejects-score-theater
evals.prompt: Review our roadmap using product management. Leadership gave every feature a reach, impact, confidence, and effort number, so the ranking is mathematically complete. The numbers are estimates with no customer or usage source, capacity is not recorded, and the roadmap contains no target segment, outcomes, non-goals, or decision checkpoints. Confirm the top five features as strategy.
evals.expected: Selects the product-decision mode, treats the unsourced scoring inputs as claims rather than evidence, refuses to promote a feature ranking into strategy, examines customer progress, strategic coherence, scope, capacity, opportunity cost, and learning gates, and recommends a decision-grade next step instead of confirming the top five.

evals.name: go-to-market-claims-cannot-outrun-proof
evals.prompt: Approve these website claims from our product brief: "Cuts operating costs by 80%, deploys in minutes, works for every enterprise, and requires no training." We have verified that the basic workflow runs, but have no measured cost outcomes, deployment timings, enterprise rollout, or adoption study. Keep the wording strong because marketing needs differentiation.
evals.expected: Selects the go-to-market-claim mode, traces each outcome, speed, universality, and ease claim to product mechanism and customer proof, marks the unsupported confidence and constraints, recommends removing, narrowing, or qualifying the claims, identifies the evidence packet marketing still needs, and preserves the handoff boundary instead of inventing persuasive proof.

evals.name: launch-readiness-requires-control
evals.prompt: Our core workflow passed a small internal pilot, so approve an immediate rollout to all regulated customers. There is no eligibility rule, permissions testing for customer roles, support owner, rollback path, value instrumentation, or review date. We will call it an experiment and watch support tickets.
evals.expected: Selects the launch-readiness mode, recognizes the higher trust and blast-radius context, fails the permissions, observability, ownership, containment, and learning gates, rejects the experiment label as a substitute for control, and recommends no broad rollout or a narrowly staged release with explicit closure and rollback conditions.
